task_summary: Characterize the IS621 bridge recombinase from sequence, predict activity-improving single mutations using multiple PLM/inverse-folding methods (ESM, ProteinMPNN, ESM-IF), build a consensus ranking that protects catalytic residues, and provide structural rationale for the top 5 candidates.

steps.title: Sequence characterization & annotation
steps.description: Confirm identity (IS621 bridge recombinase, IS110 family), compute physicochemical properties (length, MW, pI, net charge, composition), identify the N-terminal DEDD catalytic domain and C-terminal DNA-binding region, and locate the putative catalytic residues (Asp/Glu cluster) from literature. Produce a domain/annotation summary table (sequence_annotation.csv) and a residue-level feature track figure (sequence_features.png).

steps.title: Predict 3D structure with ESMFold
steps.description: Run ESMFold locally (fair-esm / huggingface) on the 326-aa sequence to generate a monomer model (is621_esmfold.pdb) with per-residue pLDDT. Save pLDDT profile plot (plddt_profile.png) and report mean/domain confidence. This structure feeds the inverse-folding methods and the structural rationale.

steps.title: ESM variant effect scoring
steps.description: Use ESM-2 (650M, and optionally ensemble with a second size) masked-marginal scoring to compute log-likelihood-ratio scores for all 19 substitutions at every position (~6,194 variants). Save full matrix esm_variant_scores.csv and a heatmap of the mutational landscape (esm_heatmap.png).

steps.title: ProteinMPNN inverse-folding scoring
steps.description: Run ProteinMPNN on the ESMFold structure to obtain per-position per-amino-acid log-probabilities; convert to mutation scores (mut logprob minus WT logprob). Save proteinmpnn_scores.csv. This is the structure-based counterpart to the sequence-based ESM scores.

steps.title: Third method (ESM-IF inverse folding) scoring
steps.description: Run ESM-IF1 (GVP-transformer inverse folding) on the ESMFold backbone as an independent structure-based predictor, giving a third orthogonal score set (esmif_scores.csv). This triangulates ESM (sequence PLM) vs ProteinMPNN and ESM-IF (two different inverse-folding architectures).

steps.title: Consensus ranking of candidates
steps.description: Merge the three score sets, z-normalize each, compute a consensus score, and apply engineering filters: exclude catalytic/metal-coordinating residues and highly conserved core positions (do-not-mutate list), favor positions with agreement across methods and reasonable pLDDT. Produce a ranked candidate table (ranked_candidates.csv) and a cross-method comparison/correlation figure (method_comparison.png).

steps.title: Structural representation of top 5 mutations
steps.description: For the top 5 candidates, render PyMOL figures showing WT vs proposed residue in structural context (distance to active site, surface vs core, local environment). Produce one labeled panel per mutation (top5_mut_<id>.png) plus an overview figure mapping all 5 onto the structure (top5_overview.png).

steps.title: Mechanistic rationale & final report
steps.description: For each top candidate, explain the hypothesized mechanism for improved activity (e.g., enhanced stability/foldability, improved DNA/RNA contact, relief of local strain, surface charge optimization) grounded in the scores and structural context. Compile ranked_candidates.csv, the figures, and a written report (engineering_report.md) summarizing findings, with large raw-data files referenced by name rather than linked.

feasibility.rationale: The sequence is confidently identified as the IS621 bridge recombinase (IS110 family). All scoring methods (ESM-2 masked marginals, ProteinMPNN, ESM-IF inverse folding) run locally on CPU, and ESMFold can generate a working structure model. Key caveat: PLM/inverse-folding scores predict fitness/foldability, which correlate with but do not directly measure catalytic turnover — so 'improved activity' candidates are hypotheses for experimental testing, and I will explicitly protect known catalytic residues from mutation.
